content.full_text:
August 27, 1976


Mr. Richard Thomson
Staff Counsel
Office of the Lieutenant Governor
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Thomson:

Thank you for your letter of August 20, 1976 regarding the
"Lieutenant Governor's Advisory Committee."

The Committee is not a lobbyist under the Act. A lobbyist under
the Political Reform Act of 1974 is one who is employed or re-
tained to communicate with any elective state official, agency
official or legislative official for the purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action. Government Code Section
82039. The Committee is not within the definition because it is
not "employed or retained" to communicate for the purpose of in-
fluencing legislative or administrative action. Based on the
facts presented in your letter, the Committee is not engaged in
lobbying activity under Government Code Section 82018(b) because
it is not making payments to influence legislative or adminis-
trative action within the meaning of Government Code Section
82045. Accordingly, there are no reporting requirements for the
Committee under Chapter 6 of the Act.

If I can be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

Sincerely,


J. RICHARD EICHMAN
Special Compliance Representative

JRE:mak
bcc: Jeanne

MERVYN M. DYMALLY
LIEUTENANT GOVERNOR

State of California

916-445-9533

OFFICE OF THE LIEUTENANT GOVERNOR
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

August 20, 1976

Richard Eichman
Fair Political Practices Commission
925 J Street
Suite 522
Sacramento, California

Dear Mr. Eichman:

I am requesting an informal opinion as to whether the organization
described below must report as a lobbyist or under any other provision
of Proposition 9.

The organization is composed of a group of citizens in the San
Francisco area which usually meets once a month for a luncheon and
guest speaker. Members pay dues which cover the cost of lunches,
mailing and secretarial work incurred in connection with the mailings.
The mailings contain only an announcement of the next meeting and a
summary of the events and the speech of the most recent meeting. The
guest speaker, usually a state legislator or constitutional officer,
receives no honorarium but does receive a free lunch and travel
expenses.

The organization is made up of business men, union officers, professional people, etc. The organization does not employ a lobbyist, takes
no political stands, supports no candidates, makes no political contributions and because of the diversity of its membership, is unlikely
to have a unified position on any piece of legislation or ballot
proposition. The dues are used solely to keep the organization going.

The speakers are chosen to inform the membership about the workings
of state government. They are not expected to speak about any particular issue, nor are they subjected to lobbying by the group in any
formal manner. However, in the informal portions of the program before and after the luncheon, it is conceivable that an individual
member would talk to the speaker about some piece of legislation or
governmental decision in which that individual had an interest.

Richard Eichman
Page 2

The name of the organization is the "Lieutenant Governor's Advisory
Committee". Its stated purpose is to provide advice to the Lieutenant
Governor on public issues. It does not benefit the Lieutenant
Governor in any other way. The Lieutenant Governor does not have
any involvement with the management of the group or its financial
affairs. He does attend some of the meetings and assists in finding
guest speakers.

Assuming that the organization spends more than $250 in a month on
the luncheon and the associated mailings, is it required to file a
monthly report as a lobbyist? Does it have any other reporting re-
sponsibilities under Proposition 9? Does any individual member
have such a responsibility?

I am grateful for your assistance in answering these questions.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Richard Thomson
Staff Counsel

RT:jt

sections.question: Assuming that the organization spends more than $250 in a month on the luncheon and the associated mailings, is it required to file a monthly report as a lobbyist? Does it have any other reporting responsibilities under Proposition 9? Does any individual member have such a responsibility?

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Lieutenant Governor's Advisory Committee, a citizen group that meets monthly for lunches with guest speakers, must register and report as a lobbyist or has other reporting obligations under Proposition 9.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from the Lieutenant Governor's office to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. Document shows formal letter requesting advice rather than providing advice. No conclusion section present as this is the inquiry, not the response.

embedding.summary: Richard Thomson, Staff Counsel for Lieutenant Governor Mervyn Dymally, requests an informal FPPC opinion on whether the Lieutenant Governor's Advisory Committee must report as a lobbyist under Proposition 9. The organization is a diverse civic group meeting monthly for informational lunches with state officials, funded by member dues for operational costs.
